metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the error-handling contract for `run_ensures()` within `--repair`: should a failure abort --repair with a non-zero exit (surfacing the error), or proceed with a logged warning (best-effort, matching MCP semantics)?
findings.finding: The error-handling semantics for `run_ensures()` when called within `--repair` are unspecified: if the ensures sweep throws, the plan does not say whether `--repair` should surface the error to the caller, swallow it, or roll back partially applied ensures — leaving an ambiguous partial-state risk.
findings.location: What section / AC line 1: '--repair additionally runs run_ensures() (folded into the existing A3 path)'
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the run_ensures() call in --repair with a try/except: log the traceback, increment a repair-failure counter, and decide explicitly whether to re-raise (aborting --repair) or continue. Document this choice in the AC or plan prose.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the error-handling contract for `run_ensures()` failures within the `--repair` path (e.g., 'log-and-continue, report failure count in repair output, do not abort A3 data remediation') analogous to the MCP server-startup fallback specification.
findings.finding: The plan folds `run_ensures()` into `fsck --repair`'s existing A3 remediation path but specifies no fallback/error-handling for the ensure portion within that path. The MCP startup path has an explicit 'log-and-continue, never abort' contract; `--repair` has no analogous spec. If `run_ensures()` raises (e.g., a failing ensure unit, a lock contention exception), it is unspecified whether the entire `--repair` run aborts, skips the ensure portion, or continues with the data-remediation.
findings.location: What section: 'FOLD run_ensures() (WS1) into the existing fsck --repair path'; AC1: 'rebar fsck --repair additionally runs run_ensures()'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the plain-fsck fallback when `.ensure-applied` is absent or unreadable: e.g., 'treat N=0 and display ensures: 0/M applied; on parse error, display ensures: ?/M applied and log a warning.'
findings.finding: Plain `rebar fsck` reads N from `.ensure-applied` but the plan does not specify the display behavior when the marker file is absent (i.e., before --repair has ever been run or on a fresh store), leaving the first-run UX undefined.
findings.location: AC line 3 / What section (read-only report line)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC line 3: 'If .ensure-applied is absent, N=0; if unreadable/corrupt, N=? with a logged warning; the report line is always emitted.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the write-lock scope for `fsck --repair`: confirm whether `write_lock` should wrap only the `run_ensures()` call (leaving existing `_repair_run` git operations unlocked) or the full `--repair` mutation path — and reconcile with `_repair_run`'s current lock-free git commits/pushes.
findings.finding: The plan instructs running `run_ensures()` under the store write lock within `fsck --repair`, but the surrounding `_repair_run()` function (which commits and pushes batches of ticket data) already mutates the store WITHOUT acquiring the write lock. Selectively locking only the ensures portion while leaving the A3 data mutation unlocked is an inconsistent, golden-hammer application of the write lock to one subset of the mutation in a single path.
findings.location: What section: 'Run the ensure-sweep under the store write lock'; fsck.py:565-674 (_repair_run — no write_lock acquisition)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the write-ordering between `run_ensures()` marker writes and tracker-branch commits within `--repair`, and state the system's schema/marker state after an abort (push failure), including whether the `pre-a3-remediation` tag covers ensure-produced commits and how a re-run detects+recovers the inconsistency.
findings.finding: If run_ensures() partially completes inside --repair and the repair subsequently ABORTs (e.g., push failure at _repair_run line 654), the .ensure-applied marker may have already been written with some applied IDs while the corresponding tracker-branch commits remain unpushed — or the marker may be absent while ensures-produced commits are already committed to the branch. The plan never specifies the write-ordering between (a) writing .ensure-applied and (b) committing/pushing ensures-produced tracker changes, so a partial failure leaves marker state and tracker-branch state irrecoverably inconsistent with no stated recovery path.
findings.location: What section ('Run the ensure-sweep under the store write lock'); AC1 ('git-level idempotent: two runs on a converged store produce zero new commits/pushes')
findings.suggested_fix: Commit ensures-produced tracker changes inside the existing batch loop (so they are covered by the pre-tag and push-fail ABORT), and write .ensure-applied only after the batch containing those commits has been successfully pushed. Document this ordering in the AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assert the expected schema/marker state of `.ensure-applied` after a `git reset --hard pre-a3-remediation` rollback of the tracker branch, and specify whether a post-rollback re-run of `--repair` correctly detects and re-applies ensures (e.g., by cross-checking marker against live tracker state, not just the marker alone).
findings.finding: The .ensure-applied marker is git-ignored (per WS1 design, it lives at the repo root and is not committed to the tracker branch). A `git reset --hard pre-a3-remediation` on the tracker branch rolls back data-repair commits but leaves .ensure-applied on disk with previously-applied IDs. The plan never asserts the system state after this rollback: the marker claims ensures are applied, but the tracker branch no longer contains their committed effects, creating a permanently inconsistent state with no recovery path stated.
findings.location: What section; AC1; Scope ('In: … no_mutate gating')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reference or reproduce the `.ensure-applied` parse contract in WS3's What section (e.g., 'parse as a JSON array and take len()'), so WS3's read path is unambiguously specified without requiring an implementer to read WS1.
findings.finding: The read-only `ensures: N/M applied` report line depends on parsing the `.ensure-applied` marker ('N = count present in the parsed .ensure-applied marker (WS1 format)'), but the plan does not specify the parse protocol for WS3's reader — only that the format is 'WS1 format'. The serialization (JSON array? newline-delimited IDs? set? list?) is defined in WS1/WS2 (both out-of-scope for WS3). An implementer of the WS3 read path has no specification for how to parse N from the marker.
findings.location: What section: 'N = count present in the parsed .ensure-applied marker (WS1 format)'; Scope: 'Out: the registry/run_ensures (WS1), the write-path hint (WS2)'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit observability requirement for the ensures portion of `--repair`: at minimum, a structured log line on success ('ensures sweep: N applied') and on failure ('ensures sweep failed: <reason>'), matching the visibility standard set for MCP startup.
findings.finding: The plan adds a new failure point — `run_ensures()` executing under the write lock inside `--repair` — but specifies no logging or instrumentation for this path (only MCP startup specifies 'log-and-continue'). Operators have no visibility into ensures failures during a --repair run.
findings.location: What section / AC line 1 and 'Run the ensure-sweep under the store write lock'
findings.suggested_fix: In the A3 path, after run_ensures() completes (or in the except block), emit a structured log entry at INFO (success) or WARNING (failure) level that includes the number of ensures applied and any exception message.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly name the byte-parity test file (`tests/interfaces/facades/test_cli_argparse.py::test_help_byte_parity`) and both golden files (`tests/golden/cli_help/fsck__help.out` AND `src/rebar/_cli/help/fsck.txt`) as the concrete edit targets for the fsck output change.
findings.finding: The plan says 'update the fsck byte-parity tests (WS3 OWNS these)' and 'fsck byte-parity + help golden(s) updated' but never names the actual test file. The byte-parity test for fsck help is `tests/interfaces/facades/test_cli_argparse.py::test_help_byte_parity`, which reads from `tests/golden/cli_help/fsck__help.out` and `src/rebar/_cli/help/fsck.txt`. An executor searching for 'byte-parity tests' could be misled by the stale cross-reference `tests/interfaces/test_e4_fsck.py` in the `fsck.py` module docstring (line 15) — that file does not exist.
findings.location: What section ('Update the fsck help golden(s)...and the fsck byte-parity tests'); AC5
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope 'In' list or AC5, add: 'Update `src/rebar/_cli/help/fsck.txt`, `tests/golden/cli_help/fsck__help.out` (and matching `-dashhelp` variant), validated by `tests/interfaces/facades/test_cli_argparse.py::test_help_byte_parity`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove the conditional 'if the report line changes help/usage' from the What section and make the fsck golden + byte-parity update obligation unconditional, matching AC #5.
findings.finding: The 'What' section conditions help-golden updates on 'if the report line changes help/usage', but AC #5 states unconditionally that 'fsck byte-parity + help golden(s) updated and owned by WS3'. Since the new 'ensures: N/M applied' line is always added to plain-fsck output, the byte-parity test always changes, making the 'if' in What contradict the definitive AC #5.
findings.location: What section ('Update the fsck help golden(s) if the report line changes help/usage') vs AC #5
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'Update the fsck help golden(s) if the report line changes help/usage' to 'Update the fsck help golden(s) and byte-parity tests to reflect the new ensures: N/M applied report line.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the lock-contention behavior for MCP startup vs. concurrent --repair: e.g., 'MCP startup uses a non-blocking try-acquire; if the lock is held, log a warning and skip the sweep (best-effort semantics preserved).'
findings.finding: The plan does not specify what happens when both MCP server startup and a concurrent `rebar fsck --repair` attempt to acquire the store write lock simultaneously — whether the second caller blocks, times out, skips the ensure sweep, or errors.
findings.location: What section (MCP server startup wiring paragraph) / AC line 4
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC line 4: 'If the write lock is unavailable at MCP startup, log a warning and skip run_ensures() (non-blocking try-acquire); the next --repair or startup will converge.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC4) with an end-to-end proving command that actually starts the MCP server with a deliberately broken run_ensures (e.g., patched to raise RuntimeError) and asserts: (a) the process reaches the serving state, and (b) a WARNING log line containing 'run_ensures' is emitted — e.g., via a subprocess test that checks exit code and stderr.
findings.finding: AC4 (MCP server startup) introduces a new code path that runs in production on every server boot, but no AC exercises it end-to-end (e.g., actually starting the MCP server and verifying the log-and-continue behavior when run_ensures fails). Per E6(f), a cutover to a new production path requires at least one criterion that exercises the path as it will run in production.
findings.location: AC4: 'MCP server startup runs run_ensures() after the --help check, best-effort (log-and-continue, never aborts boot), under the write lock'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate known callers of `rebar fsck --repair` (CI jobs, scripts, runbooks) and confirm they tolerate the added run_ensures() side-effect, or add a note acknowledging this behavioral expansion.
findings.finding: Existing callers of `rebar fsck --repair` depend on it performing only data remediation (rebuild snapshots, retire orphans). The plan folds run_ensures() into this path — a behavioral change — but does not enumerate or acknowledge those callers, leaving them potentially surprised by new side-effects.
findings.location: What section / Scope section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit AC or test specification for the MCP startup failure path: when `run_ensures()` raises, the server must still reach `build_server().run()` (e.g., a unit test that injects an exception into `run_ensures` and asserts the server-start call is still made).
findings.finding: The MCP server startup best-effort behavior ('log-and-continue, never aborts boot') introduces a new user-facing guarantee but the plan specifies no test for the failure path — i.e., no AC or test verifying that when `run_ensures()` raises an exception, the MCP server still starts successfully rather than crashing.
findings.location: AC: 'MCP server startup runs run_ensures() after the --help check, best-effort (log-and-continue, never aborts boot), under the write lock'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: 'A unit test patches `run_ensures` to raise and confirms `build_server().run()` is still called (or that the MCP server process does not exit), validating the best-effort/log-and-continue guarantee.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit prerequisite statement to WS3 (e.g., 'Requires WS1 merged: `run_ensures()` in `src/rebar/ensures.py` and `.ensure-applied` marker format are WS1 deliverables that WS3 consumes') so the dependency is tracked as a gate, not a silent assumption.
findings.finding: The plan's AC3 specifies 'M = count of registry ids (from `ensures.py`)' and multiple ACs depend on `run_ensures()` being callable — but neither `ensures.py` nor `run_ensures` exist anywhere in `src/`. They are WS1 deliverables scoped Out of WS3. The plan provides no stated prerequisite (e.g., 'WS1 must be merged before WS3 begins') and no guard in any AC against WS1 being absent. Under the rubric's fail-closed rule, an unverifiable assertion in an AC is a gap.
findings.location: Scope section ('OUT: the registry/run_ensures (WS1)'); AC1, AC2, AC3, AC4 all invoke run_ensures()

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Rename the plan title to remove '--fix' (e.g., 'WS3: rebar fsck --repair ensure-fold + ensures: N/M report line + MCP server-startup wiring') so the title matches the no-new-flag decision in the body and ACs.
findings.finding: The plan title says 'rebar fsck --fix' but the body explicitly states 'NO new --fix flag is introduced' and folds the work into '--repair'. An executor reading only the title or a ticket summary will plan for a flag that must not be created.
findings.location: Title: 'WS3: rebar fsck --fix ...' vs. body: 'NO new --fix flag'
findings.suggested_fix: Change title to: 'WS3: rebar fsck --repair ensure-fold (ensures: N/M report line, read-only default) + MCP server-startup wiring'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command (or deterministic assertion sequence) to each AC — e.g., AC1: `rebar fsck --repair 2>&1 | grep -F 'ensures:'`; AC2: run `--repair` twice on a converged store, then `git -C <store> log --oneline -1` and assert HEAD SHA is unchanged between runs; AC3: `rebar fsck | grep -E 'ensures: [0-9]+/[0-9]+ applied'`; AC4: inject a broken `run_ensures` mock and assert `mcp_server.main` exits 0 with a log line; AC5: `pytest tests/test_fsck_golden.py -v` exits 0.
findings.finding: No AC has a concrete proving command. An executor cannot deterministically confirm completion for any of the five ACs without guessing what to run.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section — all five ACs

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan or expand-contract sequence for (a) the behavioral change to '--repair' (now runs ensures in addition to data remediation) and (b) the fsck output format change (new 'ensures: N/M applied' line), addressing how consumers can revert or opt out if the new behavior causes problems.
findings.finding: The plan folds new behavior (run_ensures) into the existing '--repair' flag and adds a new line to plain fsck output, both of which are behavioral changes consumers rely on, but provides no rollback/back-out plan or expand-contract sequence — only an inline justification for the --repair fold.
findings.location: What section, AC #1, AC #5

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief ADR or CLAUDE.md note explaining why `--repair` merges data-remediation and config-ensure convergence (rather than a separate flag), so the design intent survives the PR.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a significant architectural decision — merging config-ensure convergence into the data-remediation `--repair` verb — but specifies no ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design-doc update to explain why these two concerns are co-located rather than separated. Future maintainers have no recorded rationale.
findings.location: What section / Scope section: 'folding run_ensures into --repair'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short section to CLAUDE.md (or an ADR file) titled 'Why --repair runs both data and ensure sweeps': record that a separate flag was rejected to avoid collision, that both classes of mutation require the write lock, and that a converged store is the shared postcondition.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the plan title to remove `--fix` and reflect the actual interface (e.g., 'rebar fsck --repair [ensures-fold] + read-only ensures report + MCP startup wiring') so title and body are consistent.
findings.finding: The plan title promises a `--fix` flag ('rebar fsck --fix') but the body explicitly rejects it ('NO new --fix flag', 'NO new mutation flag is introduced'). An implementer reading only the title would build the wrong CLI interface.
findings.location: Plan title vs. What section / AC line 1
findings.suggested_fix: Rename title to: 'WS3: rebar fsck --repair (fold ensures), read-only ensures: N/M report, MCP server-startup wiring'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (with a Grep/Read of `fsck.py`) that the `--repair` path acquires the store write lock, or explicitly specify in the What/AC sections that WS3 must ADD a write_lock context manager wrapping the ensure sweep within the `--repair` block.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `run_ensures()` should run 'under the store write lock' within the `--repair` path, but the existing `fsck.py` `--repair` block (lines 724–742) does NOT acquire any write lock — the `_repair_run()` call has no `write_lock` context manager. `write_lock` exists in `src/rebar/_store/lock.py` and is used in `event_append.py`, `sync.py`, `push.py`, and `txn.py`, but is entirely absent from `fsck.py`. The plan's claim that the ensure sweep can run 'under the store write lock' inside this path is unverified: either the lock must be newly added to `fsck.py` (unspecified work) or the plan misstates the integration context.
findings.location: What section ('Run the ensure-sweep under the store write lock'); AC1 ('under the store write lock'); AC4 ('under the write lock')
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section, change 'Run the ensure-sweep under the store write lock' to 'Add a `with write_lock(tracker):` context manager around the `run_ensures()` call inside the `if do_repair:` block in `fsck_cli`' — and mirror this in AC1 so the implementer knows they must add the lock, not merely use a pre-existing one.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `src/rebar/_lib_reads.py` and `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py` as consumers of `fsck_cli` affected by the new `ensures: N/M applied` report line, and confirm whether the report line is emitted (or suppressed) when `no_mutate=True`.
findings.finding: `_lib_reads.py` and `_cli/__init__.py` are callers of `fsck_cli` OUTSIDE `_commands/` that the plan does not enumerate. `_lib_reads.py:274` calls `fsck_cli([], repo_root=repo_root, no_mutate=report_only)` and is the library's read-only fsck surface; `_cli/__init__.py:260` calls `fsck_cli(rest)` as the CLI entry point. The plan adds the `run_ensures()` sweep inside `fsck_cli`'s `--repair` branch and a new `ensures: N/M applied` report line to the plain read-only path — both changes affect output read by these callers. Neither file is named in the Scope or What sections.
findings.location: What / Scope sections; AC3 ('library `no_mutate=True` surfaces stay read-only')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the What section: '`_lib_reads.py` (library fsck surface, `no_mutate=True`) and `_cli/__init__.py` (CLI router) call `fsck_cli` and will transparently inherit the new `ensures: N/M` report line; no changes needed to those files since `no_mutate=True` already gates mutation and the report line is read-only.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Rename the plan title to match the actual implementation ('WS3: rebar fsck --repair ensure-fold + MCP server-startup wiring') to eliminate the pink-elephant '--fix' antipatter.
findings.finding: The plan title says 'rebar fsck --fix' but the body explicitly states 'NO new --fix flag is introduced' and folds everything into '--repair'. An implementer reading the plan title (or a ticket summary) will name or look for a '--fix' flag that must not exist — a classic pink-elephant antipatter that will cause confusion.
findings.location: Plan title: 'WS3: rebar fsck --fix (read-only default, reports ensures: N/M)' vs. What section: 'NO new `--fix` flag'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the title to 'WS3: rebar fsck --repair (ensure-fold, reports ensures: N/M) + MCP server-startup wiring'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the `no_mutate` gating interface for `run_ensures()` in the What section — either as an explicit parameter (`run_ensures(store, no_mutate=no_mutate)`) or as a documented call-site guard (`if not no_mutate: run_ensures(...)`) so every call site implements the contract identically.
findings.finding: The plan specifies 'Honor no_mutate (library read-only surfaces never run the sweep)' but never defines how the `no_mutate` signal is passed into `run_ensures()` — whether it is a parameter (`run_ensures(no_mutate=True)`), an internal environment check, or a call-site guard in `fsck_cli`. The existing `fsck_cli` has `no_mutate: bool` at its signature; without specifying how that gates the `run_ensures` call, an implementer may skip the guard or implement it inconsistently.
findings.location: What section: 'Honor no_mutate (library read-only surfaces never run the sweep)'; AC3: 'library no_mutate=True surfaces stay read-only and never sweep'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section: 'Pass no_mutate to run_ensures() as a keyword argument; run_ensures() raises ValueError or is a no-op when called with no_mutate=True, enforcing the read-only contract at the function boundary.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
